text: The surgeon uses the non-dominant hand to medialize the medial umbilical ligament, then incises the peritoneum at the intersection with the vas deferens. Dissection continues to expose the left endopelvic fascia, and the same procedure is mirrored on the opposite side. The ratio space is fully dissected down to the pubic bone, while preserving the median umbilical ligament to prevent excessive bladder mobilization.

The surgeon incises the endopelvic fascia with cold scissors laterally to the tendinous arch, first on the left and then on the right side. A stitch is placed on the anterior surface of the prostate caudally to the bladder neck, allowing the large needle driver to lift the prostate and visualize the bladder as an inverted V shape behind the prostate base. The dissection of the bladder neck is performed using a posterolateral approach, identifying the posterior vesicoprostatic angle. The bipolar grasper protects the detrusor muscle while monopolar shears extend the dissection to the midline.

The surgeon applies tension with the non-dominant hand to expose the cleavage plane, revealing longitudinal detrusor fibers. The longitudinal fibers of the urethra are identified and spared. The urethra is opened, and the prostate is lifted by applying traction on the catheter. The posterior wall of the urethra is incised to continue the posterior dissection of the bladder neck. The vas deferens are identified, dissected, and secured with a Hem-o-lok clip before being cut. The vas deferens is lifted by the fourth arm, and the seminal vesicle is dissected using sharp and blunt techniques. Metallic clips are used to minimize energy application. The posterior plane is developed while preserving the Denonvilliers' fascia, and blunt dissection is extended down to the apex and laterally to the neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon detaches and dissects the veil of Aphrodite, releasing the anterior neurovascular bundle to preserve its integrity. The neurovascular bundle falls down and laterally to the vascular pedicle. The non-dominant hand lifts the prostate, creating a ninety-degree angle with the vascular prostatic pedicle, which is then clipped and resected. This maneuver is repeated on the right side until reaching the dorsal venous complex (DVC), ensuring the neurovascular bundle is not stretched and a thick, flat veil is obtained.
The surgeon dissects and cuts the dorsal venous complex (DVC) and the pulvoprostatic ligament while preserving the periurethral tissue. A running suture is used to close the DVC.

The surgeon performs apical dissection using the collar technique to maximize urethral length and reduce the risk of positive surgical margins. A posterior reconstruction is executed with a 30 cm V-lock suture, approximating the remnants of the delombillae, vesicoprostatic muscle, and posterior urethral rhabdosphincter with three stitches from left to right. The suture continues back from right to left, incorporating the bladder neck and urethra, aligning the mucosa on both sides of the anastomosis. The second layer forms the posterior plate of the anastomosis, which is completed with a 23 cm running suture in a counterclockwise direction. A leakage test is conducted with 180 ml of saline solution.

Between February and October, twenty-two, sixty-two patients affected by low or intermediate risk prostate cancer underwent bilateral nerve sparing RARP according to our technique using the HUGO robotic system. All the procedures were completed without any intraoperative complications. Median operative time was one hundred and fifty minutes while median consult time was one hundred and twenty minutes. On average, the urethral catheter was removed on post-operative day two and the median length of stay was three days. On final pathology, ten patients had the PT-tree disease while only three patients had positive surgical margins. One month after surgery, fifty-nine percent of the patients recovered the continence while after three months, eighty-two percent were continence. In conclusion, we were able to perform our RAR technique using the novel Hugera system, achieving optimal outcomes.
